# The Value of Theoretical Focus

The worry behind most of the decisions this guide argues against is understandable. You are a PhD student in a theory-rich field. You know which frameworks reviewers care about. Leaving them out feels like an invitation to criticism. Every experienced researcher you know seems to discuss more theories than you are now being told to.

But selection always occurs. Every introduction includes some theories and excludes others; the framework gives that selection a principled basis. When you apply the two filters and write the landscape sentence, you are making the same selection that every introduction makes, but explicitly and in advance. A reviewer who asks why a theory is absent gets a specific answer, because you worked that answer out before writing a word.

The published papers you learned theoretical framing from are frequently the end product of a long revision process in which reviewers requested additional coverage and authors complied. What you see in print is a broad introduction; what you cannot see is how it started. In many cases the theoretical framing was sharper before revision began and was widened under review. The publication process tends to add width. Broad introductions in print are often the record of that process.

Starting with a clear anchor gives you a principled basis for responding to those requests. If a reviewer asks for an additional theory, you can add a sentence, explain why the theory fails the filters for this design, and defend that decision specifically. That is much harder to do if you began without a clear theoretical commitment in the first place.

For cumulative science, the consequences are real. Research accumulates across studies, and that accumulation depends on introductions that commit clearly to a theory and test it. A study that tests a named prediction, with a design capable of discriminating between competing accounts, contributes something locatable to the body of evidence about that theory. A study that mentions several frameworks without committing to any of them contributes less. Its place in the theoretical landscape is harder to identify.

The tools in this guide — the anchor sentence, the two filters, the landscape sentence, the funnel, the mirror test — are thinking tools as much as writing tools. Writing is where you find the gaps in your argument: the mechanism you had not yet specified, the step from theory to prediction that seemed obvious and turns out to need a sentence. Using the tools forces those gaps to the surface before the draft is complete, when they are still easy to close.

Go back to your manuscript. Whether you are starting a new draft or revising an existing one, the anchor sentence comes first. Run the other candidate theories through the two filters. Write the landscape sentence. Those three acts, done on paper, will do more for your theoretical framing than any number of additional paragraphs about theories that do not change your argument.
